perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party and hereby agrees that, in addition to any other relief available at law or in equity, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause regarding injunctive relief significantly weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to quickly and effectively address breaches of the agreement. Injunctive relief is a crucial remedy for protecting confidential information, and its removal makes it harder to prevent further dissemination of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 specifies the cases in which injunctive relief may be granted. By removing the explicit acknowledgment of the Disclosing Party's right to seek injunctive relief within the contract itself, the agreement becomes ambiguous and weakens the claim to such relief in court. It would be more difficult to argue that the Recipient knowingly agreed to be subject to injunctive relief if the clause is absent.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly provides the Disclosing Party the right to seek injunctive relief, while the changed text removes this right. California Civil Code § 3422 states that "a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant," especially where "pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief". By removing the explicit mention of injunctive relief, the contract weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain this remedy, particularly when monetary compensation is insufficient, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws of the State of California , without giving effect to any conflicts of laws principles that require the application of the law of a different state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to any conflicts of laws principles that require the application of the law of a different state.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware could be problematic if the contract is primarily performed or related to activities in California. This change impacts which state's laws will be used to interpret the contract, potentially undermining California's interest in governing the transaction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 establishes the basis for jurisdiction in California courts. If the contract and its performance are primarily connected to California, attempting to apply Delaware law might be challenged based on California's significant interest in the matter and lack of meaningful connection to Delaware. This could lead to disputes over which state's laws apply and potentially invalidate the choice-of-law provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.10 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The change from California to Delaware governing law creates a potential contradiction. California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 states that a California court may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the state or US constitution. If the contract performance is primarily in California, applying Delaware law could be challenged based on California's significant interest. This contradicts the principle that California has jurisdiction over matters within its borders, especially if the contract lacks a meaningful connection to Delaware, creating a legal flaw.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings , whether written or oral, relating to the subject matter of this Agreement. The parties agree that any representation, warranty or condition, written or otherwise, not expressly contained in this Agreement or in an authorized written amendment thereto shall not be enforceable by any party .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings , whether written or oral, relating to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating that any representation, warranty, or condition not expressly contained in the agreement is unenforceable introduces ambiguity. It opens the door for parties to argue that prior oral agreements or representations, even if not written in the contract, should be enforceable, potentially contradicting the purpose of an 'entire agreement' clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856 addresses the parol evidence rule, which generally prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence (like prior oral agreements) to contradict a fully integrated written contract. By removing the explicit clause about unenforceability of unwritten terms, the contract's integration is weakened, making it easier for a party to argue that extrinsic evidence should be considered, potentially undermining the written agreement's intended finality and completeness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a clause explicitly preventing the enforcement of representations or warranties not included in the written agreement. The changed text removes this clause, weakening the contract's integration and potentially allowing extrinsic evidence (like prior oral agreements) to be considered, contradicting the purpose of an 'entire agreement' clause. CCP 1856(a) states that "Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement." By removing the clause about the unenforceability of unwritten terms, the contract's ability to prevent such contradictions is diminished.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect one (1) year from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from five years to one year significantly shortens the duration of protection for the Disclosing Party's confidential information. This makes it easier for the Recipient to disclose the information without breaching the agreement after only a year, potentially causing substantial harm to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. While a contract can define a shorter period for confidentiality, setting an unreasonably short period (like one year) may be seen as undermining the protection of genuinely valuable trade secrets that would normally be protected for a longer duration under the law. If the disclosed information constitutes a trade secret with a longer expected lifespan of competitive advantage, the one-year limit could be challenged as insufficient and unconscionable.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, while the Law Explanation suggests this could be problematic under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426) if the information constitutes a trade secret with a longer expected lifespan. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites CIV 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Thus, if the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret, the one-year confidentiality period could be challenged as insufficient, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees not to videotape, photograph or otherwise record any Confidential Information. The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use. Recipient shall remain liable for any fai lure by its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees not to videotape, photograph or otherwise record any Confidential Information. The Recipient shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any third parties other than to its affiliates, and its and their respective officers, directors, employees, consultants or professional adviser s (collectively, “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use. Recipient shall remain liable for any fai lure by its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement' lowers the standard of care expected of the Recipient. This weakens the protection of the Confidential Information, as the Recipient is no longer explicitly obligated to protect the information with at least a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the general duty of care, requiring individuals to exercise reasonable care to prevent harm to others. While the contract doesn't explicitly eliminate the duty of care altogether, removing the specific clause that defines the standard of care (at least a 'reasonable standard of care') related to Confidential Information makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove a breach of contract based on negligence. Without that language, the agreement is less clear on what constitutes a breach in failing to protect the information, creating ambiguity that weakens the Disclosing Party's position should the information be mishandled due to negligence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries resul
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for the Recipient to protect Confidential Information with 'at least a reasonable standard of care', which weakens the protection. California Civil Code § 1714(a) states that 'Everyone is responsible... for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill'. By removing the contractual obligation for a 'reasonable standard of care', the contract makes it more difficult to prove a breach of contract based on negligence, as the standard of care is no longer clearly defined within the contract itself, creating a structural flaw with legal contradiction.
